reviews.review_msg: Dr. Hanson is one of the best philosophy professor at SFU! I wasn't really interested in Metaphysics and Epistemology but he has made it very fun and enlightening. Too bad it is his last semester. He's very caring and helpful. Always crack jokes to lighten up the lecture. He grades very leniently.

Phil covers everything in class. But your dealing with theorems and different philosophers methods and whether they tell you the truth!! It is an intellectual mèlé !! That said, he is very funny and approachable and you can ask him any question, he will never make you feel like it was ridiculous. If math isn't your thing, it will still be okay.

Phil is seriously the sweetest guy and one of the best philosophy profs I've had. He's understanding and tries his best to make logic as easy as possible for you. He provided a great online textbook and comprehensive notes and slides. He can be hard to follow at times but you'll learn lot from him. He is funny too. I personally respect him.
My advice to you:  go to his office hours and chat.

As insane as this course was, being METAMETAmetaphysics course, this prof was amazing at teaching this complicated (and at times overwhelming) material.  He's a really helpful, patient guy, and just committed to helping you understand the course and do well.  Great informative and sometimes amusing lecturer. Would defs take another course with him.

Loved this course.  I already love epistemology, but this course what like an epistemology-geek's heaven.  Course was intense in that it juggled a lot of material and a wide range of approaches to the topic, but it was so well handled.  Hanson is really the prof you want to go to if you want to learn (a lot)! :)
One of the best philosophy professors i have ever met. Well-prepared for almost every single lecture.

People say he likes to hear himself talk -- I think he's one of the few profs who talks in complete sentences, mind you it may be because of some minor social impairment. Loves his philosophy, genuinely fascinated by physics-based ideas for metaphysics, likes to give a great quote. Too bad he prefers papers that argue what he believes/likes most.

Phil is a great prof. He knows the material, his office door is always open, he is a fair marker and he genuinely likes to read what students write.  A couple of recommendations for doing well: go to class even if he is a bit hard to follow, and talk to him about your paper topic at office hours.  Very intelligent, I have a lot of respect for him.

It's almost impossible to follow him during lecture. His written notes he emails are excellent, but the lecture is useless. He is so all over the place. On the other hand he is a funny guy, and is so very intelligent. Fair marker, and no finals make this class easy.

Like a lot of PHIL classes, a lot of times you seem to get it or just not... Class was a reverse bell curve, so it reflected that completely. Tutorials were helpful and assignments are easy, but midterms never was enough time... It's not content you normally would use/help you outside the subject, so know what you are getting into...
Professors...please don't make participation mandatory if you just want to hear yourself talk in lecture.

I took this course some time ago and it lead me to switch majors to philosophy. Sadly, they are not all like Phil Hanson. I highly recommend that anyone interested in philosophy take your core courses from him whenever possible. Plan your schedule around it. Most professors will not read your work carefully,respectfully and provide comments. A+

Phil is a very enthusiastic & intelligent prof. I especially appreciate the overload of comments he made to all my papers. He tends to talk 95% of the time in class, leaving little room for interactive discussions, though. And like many Philosophy prof., he may never appreciate/acknowledge an argument opposite to his - but does still let you speak.
cares  a lot about what he teaches! very smart.

Phil Hanson is easily the smartest professor up at SFU (which is not as big a deal as it may sound).  I found him extremely helpful, profoundly knowledgeable, and fostered a real interest in me for Leibniz.

He's smarter and more knowledgable then the average SFU philosophy prof.(which isn't saying much),but he can get a bit testy at times and sometimes seems to give students too much credit for their often times stupid commentary. He's also a little bit of a scatterbrain, so his course is a drag.
Great prof, has real interest in what he's doing.

Borderline genius.  Will make you feel like you ingested too many paint chips as a child.  Always has time to re-explain topics, which is nice although it never ends up being any clearer than the first time you heard it.  Good guy.
He is really good at getting students to think philosophically for themselves.
Bloody cool. Speaks in such complicated terms that it is hard to tell what hes saying, but in the end it all somehow makes sense. Very helpful, and a fair, if somewhat hard, marker.
He is unbelievably intelligent. But he is a hard marker. But he also challenges you to think, and is willing to help you insofar as you are willing to learn
